title: Sweet Pea Bunk Bed
summary: A four-panel playhouse-style bunk bed — front, rear, and two side walls, each built flat before assembly — with arched windows, a rear storage shelf, and an upper bunk opening.
description:
This is the wall-panel bed itself: front, rear, and two side panels, each framed and paneled flat on a bench before the four walls ever meet. The roof, shutters, window boxes, and pergola trim shown in the original inspiration are separate add-ons layered on afterward, not part of this cut list — building the four structural panels first, on their own, is what makes those later additions optional rather than mandatory.

The arched window openings are the one place where "measure twice" isn't enough — trace the first cut arch and use that actual cut piece as the pattern for every other arch on the same panel. Two arches that are freehanded separately, even carefully, come out close but not identical, and it shows most on a matching pair of front windows. The rear panel's paneling is the other place worth a genuine choice: solid full-length boards are simplest, but joining painted scrap lengths with pocket screws into one long color-blocked board (as the original build did above its shelf) turns builder's scraps into a design feature instead of throwing them out.

The upper bunk's side opening — the notch cut into one side panel's frame — is what a child actually climbs through to reach the top mattress, so its vertical and horizontal frame pieces need to land exactly where the mattress platform sits, not wherever is visually convenient.

tools.note: Cuts every arched window opening — use the first cut piece as a pattern for matching arches.

materials.name: Pine, 1x4 or 1x6, 8 ft, for mattress slats

cutList.note: Arched cutouts for the lower windows.
One gets the arched upper window cutouts.
Notched 2-1/2" x 3/4" on center at each end.
Arched to match the window opening.
Notched 2-1/2" x 3/4" on center at each end.
Arched to match the window opening.
4 at 1x4 width, 3 at 1x6 width — matches the source build's coloring on this section.
5 solid boards, or join painted scrap lengths with pocket screws for a color-block effect.
45° cut on the long side, trimmed back 1/2" on the short side.
Three per side panel, left and right.

cutList.part: Upper side panel pieces near opening

steps.title: Build the front frame
steps.body: Attach the front vertical bed posts to the top horizontal brace with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws from the underside and glue, keeping the posts turned so their narrow edge faces the front. Attach the remaining two horizontal braces on the inside of the posts, wide face out — the middle brace 25-1/2" down from the top brace, the bottom brace 30-1/2" down from the middle, screwed and glued from the back so the boards sit flush with the front edge of the posts. Attach the two lower-opening vertical braces 26" in from each post's inside edge, leaving a 25" center opening. Check every board for level.

steps.title: Add the mattress cleats and front inside trim
steps.body: Attach the 2x2 bed rails to the inside of the frame's bottom and middle horizontal braces — the bottom rail flush with the bottom board's bottom edge, and the middle rail flush with the top of the middle board, for extra headroom in the lower bunk. Attach the front lower and upper trim pieces to the vertical posts, wide edge out, flush with the front face.

steps.title: Cut the front window and door arches
steps.body: If you want arched windows, mark the arch locations on the upper panel piece and door opening per your own layout, using a large round object as a template for a clean, repeatable curve. Cut the first arch with a jigsaw, then use that cut piece as the pattern for every remaining arch on the same panel — freehanding each one separately almost never comes out identical.

steps.title: Build the window sills and trim
steps.body: Notch a 2-1/2" x 3/4" rectangle on center at each end of the lower and upper window sills, and dry-fit into the window openings — expect to do some fitting. Glue and nail the sills into place. Trim out each window's sides with 1x2 material, then the arched top trim, keeping edges flush and gluing and nailing throughout.

steps.title: Assemble the front panel
steps.body: Attach all the lower and upper front panel boards, window edge panels, and center upper panel pieces to the frame. Fill nail holes, sand any rough edges, and touch up paint before moving on — the front panel is now complete.

steps.title: Build the rear frame and trim
steps.body: Build the rear frame identically to the front frame — vertical posts, top brace, then the middle brace 25-1/2" down and the bottom brace 30-1/2" down from that, screwed and glued flush with the outside post edges. Attach the rear bed rails and the rear lower and upper trim the same way as the front.

steps.title: Panel the rear wall
steps.body: Dry-fit the rear panel boards against the trimmed frame. For the upper section, either use solid boards or join painted scrap lengths at random with pocket screws to create a color-blocked panel — keep board width consistent within each row. Turn the assembled panel over, drill pocket holes along the top and bottom edges of each row roughly every 15", alternating placement row to row, then glue and screw the whole panel together before standing it up and screwing it to the frame.

steps.title: Add the rear shelf
steps.body: Cut eight shelf brackets from 1x4 stock: mark 3-1/2" for the long side, cut a 45° angle, then trim 1/2" off the point with a straight 90° cut to leave a small ledge. Find the third board down from the top of the rear panel, mark 15" in from each end, and attach the first two brackets flush with that board's top edge using a nail gun and glue. Mark another 15" in from those and attach the remaining two. Glue the shelf boards to the panel and brackets and nail in place.

steps.title: Build the two side panels
steps.body: Assemble the frame pieces for each side panel, screwing rather than gluing the frame joints so the whole bed can still be disassembled and moved through a doorway later. Add the upper bunk opening's vertical and horizontal 2x4 pieces to one side panel only, positioned where the child will climb through to the top bunk.

steps.title: Trim and panel the side walls
steps.body: Attach the upper and lower side panel trim flush against the outside edges of both side frames. Using glue and a nail gun, panel each side from the inside, working top to bottom — the upper sections use the narrower side-panel pieces near the bunk opening, and the lower sections alternate wide and narrow boards down to the bottom of the bed.

steps.title: Assemble the four walls
steps.body: Attach the three shorter walls to the back wall with 2-1/4" screws and glue at the leg joints, then attach the front wall to the side walls. Check the whole assembly for square as you go.

steps.title: Add the mattress slats
steps.body: Lay the mattress slats across the bed rails, spaced 8–10" apart and lined up along the rails. Screw down to secure. Fill any exposed fastener holes, sand every surface, and prime and paint the whole structure.
materials: Pine, 1x4 or 1x6, 8 ft, for mattress slats

images.alt: Playhouse-style bunk bed with arched windows and a rear storage shelf
